Shows intrinsic motivation (interest, mastery, autonomy) predicts performance, persistence, and well-being more robustly than extrinsic rewards like salary. Supports the "follow interesting work" view, but emphasizes autonomy and competence, not just interest.

**"If We're So Rich, Why Aren't We Happy?" — Easterlin (1995); and Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being"**
Income increases life satisfaction with diminishing returns; beyond a threshold, extra salary contributes little to daily happiness. Weakens the case for switching primarily for higher pay.

**"So Good They Can't Ignore You" — Cal Newport (2012, book, not a paper)**
Argues "follow your passion" is bad advice; rare and valuable skills generate passion, autonomy, and interesting work over time. A counterpoint: your *current* interesting role may itself be the rare asset; leaving it has a real cost.

**"The Peak-End Rule" and research on memory vs. experience — Kahneman**
We mispredict what will make us happy, often overweighting salient features (title, salary, "leading a team") and underweighting day-to-day experience. Suggests weighting the *daily* texture of the work (which you describe as "much less interesting") heavily.

**"Managerial transitions" literature — e.g., Linda Hill, "Becoming a Manager" (HBR/book)**
First-time management is often disorienting and less satisfying than anticipated, particularly for technical experts who lose hands-on work. Tempering the appeal of "leading a small team."

**Net read:** the research broadly supports your friends' direction — intrinsic interest and daily engagement are underrated relative to salary and titles — but "interesting" isn't the *only* axis; autonomy, skill growth, and whether management itself becomes intrinsically rewarding matter too.
